text:
A radical new exploration of the nature of war in the 21st century Global Policing Wars explores the dynamics of military intervention in the post-Cold War era through the prism of the US wars in Afghanistan and Iraq. Going beyond the level of military strategy and the politics of individual interventions, the book advances a new interdisciplinary approach that draws on social theory and philosophy to understand how war is imagined and practiced as part of liberal governance. Holmqvist argues that liberal politics and theory has redefined war and peace as an administrative matter of governance, an ongoing business of policing what it deems to be “abnormal” or “criminal” and “lawful” and “orderly”. The enemy, whose political legitimacy is contested, is reduced to a criminal who must be punished. The resulting “policing wars” is a new mode of war that operates beyond the conventional “war” vs. “peace” or “war” vs. “crime” oppositions and has been increasingly regularized in Western military doctrine and practice.

Liberal Interventionism and Crisis Governance Scaffolding Time, Space and Power in the Liberal War on Terror tells the intellectual history of the liberal war on terror, from its roots in the liberal peace debate in the 1990s, through to the intervention in Afghanistan after 9/11, to contemporary governance in crisis contexts in Libya and Syria. By tracing this development of liberal war from its early stages, through to its fully fledged post-adversarial incarnation today, the book reveals the myth of a post-adversarial war and the unintended consequences this myth produces. Through a nuanced examination of the liberal imagination, Scaffolding Time, Space and Power in the Liberal War on Terror contributes to the emerging scholarship in the field of critical war studies by exploring the implications of the liberal imagination on the logics of organised violence in the contemporary world, and in so doing, sheds light on the evolution of global governance as a liberal project.
